sources.title: Andrew Yang: UBI Before UHI, Solving Job Loss, and the Future of Work
All-In E259
sources.context: Calacanis revealed he deployed AI agents (using open-source Claudebot/Maltbot framework) across his venture firm in 72 hours, creating virtual employees called 'replicants'. Episode also covers Kimi K2.5 open-source model launch and implications for AI costs and deployment.

sources.title: All-In E261
sources.context: Topic 1: AI Acceleration. Discussion triggered by UC Berkeley/HBR study on AI tool adoption at a 200-person tech company. Covers enterprise adoption, agent deployment, token economics, on-prem vs cloud, and data security.

sources.title: OpenClaw: The Viral AI Agent that Broke the Internet
sources.context: Steinberger is the creator of OpenClaw (formerly Claudbot/Moldbot), the fastest-growing GitHub repo ever (175K+ stars). OpenClaw is an open-source AI agent framework that lives on your computer and uses messaging clients as its interface. Briu uses OpenClaw as its agent platform.

sources.title: In 36 months, the cheapest place to put AI will be space
sources.context: Auto-caption transcript, cleaned. Speakers inferred from context — interviewers are the Collison brothers (Stripe founders).

sources.title: OpenClaw Explained: Baby AGI, Security Threats, Mac Mini Became Everyone's Supercomputer

speakers.alex_finn: Alex Finn (OpenClaw power user, YouTube creator)

sources.title: Lex Fridman Podcast #333
sources.context: Recorded before the ChatGPT explosion. Karpathy had recently left Tesla as Director of AI. GPT-3 existed but ChatGPT had not yet launched. This makes many quotes remarkably prescient.

quotes.quote: I've spent the last 72 hours doing this. I'm going to explain to you what we did at the company. It's mind-blowing.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Validates Briu's rapid deployment promise. A sophisticated VC firm went from zero to operational agents in 72 hours. Proves this isn't theoretical — it's happening now at real companies.

quotes.quote: You run this like a server. Then you start authenticating it with your services. So, Gmail, Notion, Slack, WhatsApp, maybe even your password manager.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Maps directly to Briu's integration story — connecting agents to existing business tools (Gmail, Notion, Slack). Shows the integration layer is the real value.

quotes.quote: We created like basically a virtual employee. We put it together and we made it the producer for my new This Week in AI podcast.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Frames agents as virtual employees with real personas and real accounts — exactly how Briu positions agent deployment. Not a chatbot, a colleague.

quotes.quote: We said make a CRM for all potential guests and suggest other guests and it vibe coded a CRM for itself.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Powerful proof point: the agent didn't just use existing tools — it built its own tooling. Supports Briu's narrative that agents create compounding value.

quotes.quote: This is as good as like Nick or Lisa would do at a first pass, but it did it instantly.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Named-quality comparison to real employees. 'As good at first pass, but instant.' Perfect for Briu's speed-vs-quality messaging.

quotes.quote: All the stuff that a producer would do over a day or two — do a timeline, give suggested questions. So I teach it this.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Shows the teaching/prompting workflow. A day or two of human work compressed into minutes. Supports Briu's efficiency claims.

quotes.quote: It guessed his email, sent it. I said, 'CC Heidi.' I haven't given it access to my stuff. And it said, 'Oh, do you want to get his top three times?' It's crazy what's going on here, folks.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Shows agents proactively anticipating needs — not just executing commands but thinking ahead. Supports Briu's 'agents that learn' positioning.

quotes.quote: Out of 50 hours a producer does a week, this does 40 of them. And of what an SDR does, this does 95%.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: The headline stat. Producer: 80% automated (40/50 hours). SDR: 95% automated. Perfect for comparison charts and hero stats on the Briu site.

quotes.quote: We created a group called replicants. So now we've made five replicants at the company. And today is like replicant day or replicant week.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Shows a real company going from one agent to five in days. Supports Briu's phased rollout approach — start with one, scale fast.

quotes.quote: So we started one to be an SDR and then we are giving it access to our CRM there. But it built its own CRM, David. It's building its own SaaS tools to solve its problems.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Agents that build their own tools. This is the compounding intelligence story — each integration makes the agent smarter and more capable.

quotes.quote: Every time you add it to something, it gets smarter.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Perfect tagline-quality quote. Supports Briu's 'compounding value' narrative — agents improve with each new connection and task.

quotes.quote: The LinkedIn is going to start adding people. People don't know that these aren't humans.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Use carefully — highlights both the power and the ethical edge. Briu can position as the responsible deployment partner that sets appropriate boundaries.

quotes.quote: I told it every time you do work for me in the replicant room, say what you did and then also put it on your calendar. So now it's putting on its calendar what it did in timestamps.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Self-documenting agents with audit trails. Maps to Briu's transparency and trust messaging — you can see everything the agent does.

quotes.quote: The crazy thing is you can start talking to it directly. I can have a direct conversation with Producer X and it learns and now it knows everything.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Natural language interaction with agents. Employees don't need technical skills — they just talk to the agent like a colleague.

quotes.quote: This will be the year of the rise of the personal AI assistant. Until now, AI has mainly come in the form factor of a chatbot and it's been used as a research tool. Now, we're moving to a completely different form factor — an AI assistant that's an agent that can do things for you.
quotes.speaker: Sacks
quotes.briu_use: Sacks framing the chatbot-to-agent transition. Validates Briu's core thesis: we're past the chatbot era, into the agent era. Coming from a policy-connected voice (now US CTO).

quotes.quote: Everyone's going to have this amazing AI agent.
quotes.speaker: Sacks
quotes.briu_use: Simple, declarative prediction from someone now in government. Supports Briu's urgency messaging — this is inevitable, the question is when you start.

quotes.quote: It's not just the assistant in the way that you might otherwise be thinking about it — it's like this super worker. The super worker does scheduling, calendaring, ideation, knowledge work, creates new code, creates CRM tools, books your travel. It does everything.
quotes.speaker: Chamath
quotes.briu_use: Chamath's 'super worker' framing is premium Briu content. Enumerate capabilities to show breadth. The word 'everything' lands hard.

quotes.quote: If you start thinking about having an army of super workers, you're like, 'Oh my god, what's possible?'
quotes.speaker: Chamath
quotes.briu_use: The scaling vision. One agent is interesting. An army of agents is transformative. Supports Briu's 'start with one, scale to many' approach.

quotes.quote: That's the future where everyone has a super workforce and you put two Optimuses in your garage and they build and run a business for you and then everyone becomes an entrepreneur.
quotes.speaker: Chamath
quotes.briu_use: The democratization vision. AI agents lower the barrier to running a business. Briu helps businesses get this future today — digital agents before physical ones arrive.

quotes.quote: It keeps learning. Now, your API bill is going to be nuts. The first day we did this, we hit like $100. I think it's going to be like $1,000 a day in API calls.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Real cost data from real deployment. $100/day on day one, projected $1,000/day at scale with closed-source APIs. Sets up the cost problem that open source and Briu's optimization solve.

quotes.quote: My team's like, 'Hey, this is a lot of API calls because people are going crazy inside Slack and making all of these agents.'
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Shows organic adoption — once people see agents work, they want more. Also highlights the cost management challenge that Briu helps solve.

quotes.quote: We are ordering Mac Studios, the really powerful ones with max memory, and we're putting Kimi on them. We're going to have it free. According to people online, 95% of all these queries can be done for free with Kimi.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: The cost arbitrage play: route 95% of queries to local open-source models, pay API costs only for the hard 5%. This is the economics Briu can deliver for clients.

quotes.quote: You'll save 90%.
quotes.speaker: Chamath
quotes.briu_use: Two-word cost reduction claim from Chamath. Chart-worthy: closed-source API costs vs. open-source local inference. 90% savings.

quotes.quote: You're going to cut the cost of AI by 90%. And when you do that, your bill is going to be 10 bucks a day.
quotes.speaker: Chamath
quotes.briu_use: From $1,000/day to $10/day. That's the chart. Closed-source vs. optimized open-source deployment. This is Briu's value proposition in one stat.

quotes.quote: I cancelled all of my OpenAI accounts. $25,000 gone.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: A VC spending $25K on OpenAI walked away. Shows the economic shift is real and happening at the top. Supports Briu's cost optimization narrative.

quotes.quote: This is independent of whatever LLM you want to use. You can swap out LLMs. You could route it to different LLMs. You can do whatever you want with it.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Model-agnostic architecture. Maps directly to Briu's approach — no vendor lock-in, swap models as better ones emerge. Future-proof by design.

quotes.quote: I don't think people understand how important this Kimi K2.5 moment is.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: General urgency quote. The open-source inflection point changes everything about cost, control, and deployment speed.

quotes.quote: I wanted to set it up really badly, but I was too afraid of the security issues to do it.
quotes.speaker: Sacks
quotes.briu_use: Even sophisticated tech investors hesitate on security. This is exactly the gap Briu fills — handling the security, permissions, and trust layer so clients don't have to worry.

quotes.quote: Nobody like Claude or XAI or Microsoft would ever allow you to do this because it's so dangerous, obviously.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Acknowledges the real risk. Briu's value is navigating this danger responsibly — setting up proper sandboxing, permissions, and approval gates.

quotes.quote: You're one terms of service update away from everything breaking. Because at one point, Anthropic didn't like what was going on and they said, 'No, this is not allowed.'
quotes.speaker: Chamath
quotes.briu_use: Platform risk with closed-source. Supports Briu's approach of building on open-source and self-hosted infrastructure for resilience.

quotes.quote: Open source is important because it's transparent. It gives everybody their own sovereignty. It gives you control of your own speed. You can audit the weights of the models. It allows you to host it on your own hardware. And the most important thing is the data never leaves your control.
quotes.speaker: Chamath
quotes.briu_use: Chamath's case for open source maps to Briu's data sovereignty pitch. 'The data never leaves your control' is a client-facing talking point.

quotes.quote: People are starting to daisy chain the Mac Studios. This is commodity hardware running open source. We have control of all the data. We have it on our own hardware. We can run it infinitely.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: The infrastructure play: commodity Apple hardware + open-source models = infinite inference at fixed cost. Briu can recommend this setup for appropriate clients.

quotes.quote: 90% or 95% of our jobs are going to go to this local hardware we'll control. We don't have to worry about our information going up to Sam.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: 95% local / 5% cloud split. This is the hybrid architecture Briu can deploy. Privacy-first with cloud fallback for complex tasks only.

quotes.quote: There are papers that indicate we could probably go down by 70 to 100x in terms of compute need in how the model actually runs. So yeah, ultimately these things end up on the iPhone running locally.
quotes.speaker: Friedberg
quotes.briu_use: 70-100x compute reduction on the horizon. Supports Briu's message that costs are plummeting and now is the time to build the workflow layer while models get cheaper underneath.

quotes.quote: Somebody has to be able to take an open source model and sandbox it. We're going to run it under all these race conditions. We're going to get to all these corner cases so that we can tell you that it's actually good to go and then you can use it.
quotes.speaker: Chamath
quotes.briu_use: The validation and trust gap Chamath identifies is a business opportunity. Briu can position as the team that does this work — vetting, sandboxing, and certifying agent deployments.

quotes.quote: You're going to need AI models to look for corrupted code and to do the security eval. And there's going to have to be continuous monitoring.
quotes.speaker: Sacks
quotes.briu_use: Continuous monitoring as a requirement. Supports Briu's ongoing management proposition — deployment isn't the end, it's the beginning.

quotes.quote: When you farm out work, the proprietary models keep that agentic layer kind of secret. Kimi K2.5 was like, 'Okay look, here's this thing called Agent Swarm' — a technology that allows you to create 100 sub-agents that solve any complicated multi-step problem in parallel.
quotes.speaker: Chamath
quotes.briu_use: Agent Swarm architecture — 100 parallel sub-agents. This is the orchestration layer Briu builds for clients. Multi-step, multi-agent workflows.

quotes.quote: The people I'm talking to in startups, which is where you always see the most efficient use of technology, they're all using Kimi.
quotes.speaker: Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Startups as leading indicators. If the most efficient companies are already on open-source AI, larger companies need to follow. Briu bridges that gap.

quotes.quote: It widens the aperture of what's possible. It's not just the assistant in the way that you might otherwise be thinking about it.
quotes.speaker: Chamath
quotes.briu_use: Reframing what AI means. People think 'calendar assistant' but it's a full super worker. Briu helps clients see and capture the full aperture.

quotes.quote: It's a matter of time until the big model makers create an incremental revenue stream for guys like you to license back all your prompt and response data. And you'll probably make enough to pay for all the costs of hosting and running these models.
quotes.speaker: Chamath
quotes.briu_use: Speculative but interesting — data as revenue. Supports Briu's advisory role in helping clients think about their AI data strategy long-term.

quotes.quote: My most contrarian belief is that AI would increase demand for knowledge workers, not put them out of business.
quotes.speaker: David Sacks
quotes.briu_use: Counter-narrative to AI fear. Briu's positioning: we make your team more valuable, not redundant.

quotes.quote: Employees who use AI worked at a faster pace, took a broader scope of tasks, and extended work into more hours of the day. Workers reported feeling more productive.
quotes.speaker: Jason Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: HBR/UC Berkeley study data point. Validates that AI adoption increases output, not just efficiency. Good for Why Now page.

quotes.quote: We're moving from task-based jobs to purpose-based jobs. A key skill of employees is going to be the ability to structure work for themselves and their AI agents.
quotes.speaker: David Sacks
quotes.briu_use: Perfect framing for Briu's orchestrator model. Employees become directors of agent work, not replaced by it.

quotes.quote: There's a tremendous opportunity this year for employees who are early adopters of these tools — so-called AI natives. They're going to appear to have superpowers.
quotes.speaker: David Sacks
quotes.briu_use: Urgency messaging. Briu helps businesses give every employee superpowers, not just the early adopters.

quotes.quote: They can take an assignment that would have taken days before and get it done in 2 hours — whether it's a presentation or a spreadsheet.
quotes.speaker: David Sacks
quotes.briu_use: Concrete time compression example. Good for homepage or case study framing.

quotes.quote: You're going to see massive enterprise adoption of AI, not just chatbots, but agents this year. But it's going to be driven by the bottom up — early adopter employees bringing consumerized AI tools into their workplaces.
quotes.speaker: David Sacks
quotes.briu_use: Validates Briu's thesis: adoption is bottom-up, not top-down IT mandates. Briu helps businesses channel this energy productively.

quotes.quote: Every startup I know is hiring for this position — somebody who knows how to build and manage agents. There is no job rec for this yet or a title.
quotes.speaker: Jason Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: This is literally the role Briu fills. The unnamed job is what Briu does as a service — build and manage agents for companies that can't hire for it yet.

quotes.quote: It's no longer just prompt engineering. It's managing and educating and offloading work to an agent and then making sure they're actually doing it.
quotes.speaker: Jason Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Describes Briu's exact service model. Agent management as a discipline, not just a technical trick.

quotes.quote: The people in my organization — four of them out of 20 are focused on this. Their leverage is between 10 and 20x the other 16.
quotes.speaker: Jason Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: 10-20x leverage stat from a real operator. Chart-worthy: 4 AI-native employees producing 10-20x the output of 16 non-adopters.

quotes.quote: When we do, I think our leverage versus a competing firm is going to be 10x.
quotes.speaker: Jason Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Competitive advantage framing. Companies with agent infrastructure will be 10x more productive than those without.

quotes.quote: Every week 5 to 10% of the work we're doing inside of our venture firm is being moved over to OpenClaw. We call them replicants.
quotes.speaker: Jason Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Migration velocity stat. 5-10% per week means full coverage in months. Chart-worthy adoption curve.

quotes.quote: We give them a Notion account, a Slack account, and a Google Docs account. They have their own email.
quotes.speaker: Jason Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Agents as first-class team members with real tool access. Mirrors Briu's agent deployment model — agents get onboarded like employees.

quotes.quote: We've created OpenClaw Ultron — one meta replicant that is managing the other four. It checks their work, talks to them all day long about what they're doing, and then summarizes it.
quotes.speaker: Jason Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Orchestrator pattern. One supervisory agent managing specialist agents. This is the architecture Briu deploys.

quotes.quote: They use Kimmy for most of their easy jobs, which is free. Then they will use Claude 4.6 Opus to orchestrate things.
quotes.speaker: Jason Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Model tiering strategy — cheap models for routine work, premium models for orchestration. This is how Briu manages client costs.

quotes.quote: The best part about it is they don't forget to do work. They don't make mistakes. Once you put this in, you don't need to have checklists. They just do it perfectly every single time.
quotes.speaker: Jason Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Reliability messaging. Agents as consistent, error-free operators. Strong selling point for process-heavy businesses.

quotes.quote: I was able to take maybe 30% of the Athena assistant's work and give it to the replicant — let the Athena assistant work on higher level stuff.
quotes.speaker: Jason Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: 30% task offload stat. Agents handle the routine so humans do higher-value work. Perfect for Briu's 'uplevel your team' narrative.

quotes.quote: On the average investment team individual, we now have probably 20% of their work being done by agents in real time.
quotes.speaker: Jason Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: 20% of knowledge worker output already handled by agents. Chart-worthy: current agent penetration rate in a real firm.

quotes.quote: Is on-prem the new cloud? We've spent since 2008 migrating everything to cloud. In the AI revolution, companies will be fighting for their lives. It's very unclear whether it makes sense to allow the natural leakage of their confidential and proprietary information out into the wild.
quotes.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
quotes.briu_use: Data sovereignty concern. Briu can position as helping clients deploy agents with proper data controls — not just throwing data at public endpoints.

quotes.quote: If you're using a set of agents to act on all that information, all those agent traces are going back to these model builders. I suspect it is a deep problem for others and they just haven't uncovered it yet.
quotes.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
quotes.briu_use: Agent trace leakage concern. Briu can address this by deploying with proper guardrails, private instances, and data handling policies.

quotes.quote: You will need AI to survive. But if you use the tools as they exist today at a public endpoint, you will give up all control, all security, all confidentiality.
quotes.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
quotes.briu_use: Powerful tension: AI is non-optional but security is non-negotiable. Briu helps navigate this exact dilemma.

quotes.quote: We with our agents hit $300 a day per agent using the Claude API. That's $100,000 a year per agent.
quotes.speaker: Jason Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Key cost benchmark. $300/day = $100K/year per agent at peak usage. Chart-worthy comparison: agent cost vs employee salary. This is the ceiling Briu helps clients stay under.

quotes.quote: We're getting to a place where we have to basically now say what is the token budget that we're willing to give our best devs.
quotes.speaker: Jason Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Token budgets as the new headcount planning. Briu helps clients set and optimize these budgets.

quotes.quote: Now they need to be at least 2x as productive as another employee. That is actively happening inside my business, because otherwise I'll run out of money.
quotes.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
quotes.briu_use: The 2x productivity floor. If an employee with agent access isn't 2x a non-agent employee, the token spend doesn't justify itself. Briu ensures clients clear this bar.

quotes.quote: When do tokens outpace the salary of the employee? Because you're about to hit it. I'm about to hit it. Superstar developers are already there.
quotes.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
quotes.briu_use: The token-salary crossover point. This is the key economic inflection. Chart-worthy: token cost curve approaching salary line. Briu helps clients stay on the right side of this equation.

quotes.quote: The average non-technical employee is probably in the hundreds to low thousands. But the trend is what matters.
quotes.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
quotes.briu_use: Current token spend range by employee type. Useful for cost modeling and client conversations. Non-technical employees: $100s-low $1000s/month.

quotes.quote: Unless we have some gigantic leap forward in generating output tokens at one-tenth the cost of what they are today — which I suspect we will have.
quotes.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
quotes.briu_use: 10x token cost reduction prediction. Validates Briu's Why Now thesis: costs are falling fast, making agent deployment increasingly viable for every business.

quotes.quote: Nvidia and Grock and Google and AMD, they're all incentivized to massively ramp up the energy density and massively push down the token cost. That's going to happen.
quotes.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
quotes.briu_use: Hardware competition driving costs down. Supports Briu's cost curve charts showing inevitable price drops.

quotes.quote: On prem is back. It's going to happen. It's cost savings plus do I want to give all of the secrets in our organization, every piece of intellectual property, to Sam Altman?
quotes.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
quotes.briu_use: On-prem AI as a differentiator. Briu can offer deployment options that keep client data in-house.

quotes.quote: It is mind-blowing how fast this is going. This is the 100% focus of our firm.
quotes.speaker: Jason Calacanis
quotes.briu_use: Urgency and conviction from a major tech investor. Reinforces that this is not optional or experimental.

quotes.quote: One of the solutions to this is just giving each employee a really powerful desktop that is capable of running a local large language model — which right now takes a Mac Studio with 512 gig.
quotes.speaker: David Friedberg
quotes.briu_use: Local inference as privacy solution. Relevant to Briu's deployment options for security-conscious clients.

quotes.quote: 8090 is a top 20 Bedrock customer. It's too expensive already as it is because of all this overhead — all the nonsense inside of AWS that you have to pay for just to get access to the bare metal.
quotes.speaker: Chamath Palihapitiya
quotes.briu_use: Cloud AI infrastructure cost pain. Validates why Briu's approach of optimizing model selection and deployment matters for cost control.

quotes.quote: Their work was getting upleveled. They're able to offload more menial tasks to AI and it made their work more purposeful and meaningful.
quotes.speaker: David Sacks
quotes.briu_use: Employee satisfaction angle. AI doesn't just save money — it makes work more meaningful. Good for Briu's 'empowerment not replacement' messaging.

quotes.quote: I made the agent very aware — it knows what its source code is. It understands how it sits and runs in its own harness. It knows where documentation is. It knows which model it runs. It understands its own system. That made it very easy for an agent to — you don't like anything, you just prompted into existence and then the agent would just modify its own software. People talk about self-modifying software. I just built it.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Demonstrates the core architectural principle behind OpenClaw agents Briu deploys: agents that understand their own system can self-improve and adapt to client needs without manual intervention.

quotes.quote: I actually think vibe coding is a slur. I do agentic engineering — and then maybe after 3am I switch to vibe coding and have regrets the next day.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Reinforces Briu's positioning as serious agentic engineering consultancy, not 'vibe coding' hobbyists. The distinction between disciplined agent work and reckless prompting is core to Briu's value proposition.

quotes.quote: My search was literally just hooking up WhatsApp to Claude Code. One shot — the CLI message comes in, I call the CLI with minus P, it does its magic, I get the string back and I send it back to WhatsApp. And I built this in 1 hour and already felt really cool. It's like, oh, I can talk to my computer.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Illustrates how quickly functional agents can be prototyped — mirrors Briu's promise of rapid deployment. The 1-hour prototype became the fastest-growing GitHub repo ever.

quotes.quote: I sent it an audio message and it just worked. I literally wrote 'How the hell do you do that?' And it said: 'The message was a file with no file ending. So I checked the header, found it was Opus audio, used ffmpeg to convert it, then found your OpenAI key and used curl to send it to their transcription API.' You didn't teach it any of those things and the agent just figured it out.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Perfect anecdote for demonstrating agent resourcefulness — agents don't need to be pre-programmed for every scenario. They compose tools creatively. This is exactly what Briu delivers: agents that solve problems you didn't anticipate.

quotes.quote: I feel like I built my little playground. You go like level one: agentic loop. What can I do there? How can I be smart at queuing messages? How can I make it more human? Then I go to memory — you want them to remember stuff. Then you can go to level community management. Level website and marketing. There's just so many hats you have to have on.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Maps directly to Briu's service tiers — the progression from basic agentic loop to memory to specialized skills mirrors the journey Briu guides clients through.

quotes.quote: Through this whole process you're a one-man team. There's people helping, but you're doing so much of the key core development.
quotes.speaker: Fridman
quotes.briu_use: Core proof point: one person built the fastest-growing GitHub repo ever. Validates Briu's thesis that small teams with agents can achieve outsized results.

quotes.quote: In January, 6,600 commits, probably more. I'm limited by the technology of my time. I could do more if agents would be faster.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: 6,600 commits in one month by one person is a stunning productivity data point. Chart material: compare to typical team output.

quotes.quote: Depending on how much I slept and how difficult the tasks, between four and ten agents running at the same time.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Concrete number: one skilled operator running 4-10 parallel agents. This is the multiplier Briu promises clients — not replacing humans, but amplifying them.

quotes.quote: Why did you win? Because they all take themselves too serious. It's hard to compete against someone who's just there to have fun.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Aligns with Briu's 'calm, fun, no-hype' tone. The best agent work comes from genuine curiosity and play, not enterprise theater.

quotes.quote: There was this one guy who told me: 'I run this design agency and we never had custom software. Now I have 25 little web services for various things that help me in my business, and I don't even know how they work — but they work.'
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Perfect Briu case study parallel — non-technical business owner gets 25 custom tools via agents. This is exactly the value proposition Briu offers.

quotes.quote: Every time someone made their first pull request is a win for our society. It like doesn't matter how shitty it is, you got to start somewhere. I found it very meaningful that I built something that people love so much that they actually start to learn how open source works.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Agents are lowering the bar for participation in building software. Briu can frame this as democratization — clients don't need to be developers to have custom tooling.

quotes.quote: Prompt injection is still an open problem industry-wide. When you have a thing with skills being defined in a markdown file, there's so many possibilities of obvious low-hanging fruit, but also incredibly complicated and sophisticated and nuanced attack vectors.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Honest acknowledgment of the security landscape. Briu's value-add is managing these risks for clients — we understand the attack surface so they don't have to.

quotes.quote: I warn in my security documentation: don't use cheap models. Don't use Haiku or a local model. If you use a very weak local model, they are very gullible. It's very easy to prompt inject them.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Justifies Briu's recommendation to use premium models (Opus, GPT-5.3 Codex) rather than cutting corners. Security and model quality are directly correlated.

quotes.quote: The attack surface decreases as models get smarter, but then the damage they can do increases because the models become more powerful. It's this weird three-dimensional tradeoff.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Frames the security conversation maturely — not fear-mongering, but acknowledging the nuanced tradeoff. This is the kind of informed perspective Briu brings to client deployments.

quotes.quote: You can now have a sandbox. You can have an allow list. There's a lot of ways how you can mitigate and reduce the risk. If you make sure that you are the only person who talks to it, the risk profile is much much smaller. If you stick to my recommendations of having it in a private network, that whole risk profile falls away.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Concrete security best practices that Briu implements for clients: sandboxing, allow-lists, private networks, single-user access patterns.

quotes.quote: It's a skill that you have to learn like any other skill. Some people try it once — it's like you sit me on a piano, I played once and it doesn't sound good and I say the piano is broken. You have to learn the language of the agent a little bit. Understand where they're good and where they need help.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Core justification for Briu's consulting model — agentic engineering is a skill. Businesses need experienced operators, not just tools. This is why you hire Briu rather than DIY.

quotes.quote: Not a lot of people ever considered the way the agent sees the world. You start from nothing and you have a bad agent file that doesn't help them at all, and then they explore your codebase which is a pure mess with weird naming, and then people complain that the agent's not good. Like, you try to do the same if you have no clue about the codebase.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Explains why most businesses fail at agent deployment — they don't structure their environment for agent success. Briu's expertise is in creating agent-friendly architectures.

quotes.quote: Many times I just do the refactor because refactors are cheap now. Even though you might break some other PRs, nothing really matters anymore. Modern agents will just figure things out. They might just take a minute longer.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Paradigm shift: refactoring is now cheap because agents handle the tedious parts. This changes the economics of software maintenance — a key Briu selling point.

quotes.quote: I find it quite easy to work with agents because I led engineering teams before. Eventually you have to understand and accept and realize that your employees will not write the code the same way you do. Maybe it's also not as good as you would do, but it will push the project forward.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Managing agents is like managing a team. This insight is at the heart of what Briu does — we bring engineering leadership skills to agent deployment.

quotes.quote: I'm not building the codebase to be perfect for me. I want to build a codebase that is very easy for an agent to navigate. Don't fight the name they pick because it's most likely in the weights the name that's most obvious. Next time they do a search, they'll look for that name. If I decide 'oh no, I don't like the name,' I'll just make it harder for them.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Agent-first architecture design — Briu helps clients restructure their systems to be agent-navigable, which is a fundamentally different design philosophy than human-first.

quotes.quote: Half a year ago everyone was talking about MCPs. I was like, screw MCPs — every MCP would be better as a CLI. This stuff doesn't even have MCP support in the core layer and nobody's complaining. Models are really good at calling Unix commands. If you just add another CLI, that's just another Unix command. MCP has to be added in training — that's not a very natural thing for the model.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Important architectural decision for Briu's agent deployments — CLIs over MCPs. Simpler, more composable, better understood by models. This is practical engineering wisdom.

quotes.quote: If I would build a weather service as a CLI and it gives me a huge blob, the agent could just add a jq command and filter itself, then only get what it actually needs — or maybe compose it into a script to do calculations and only give me the actual output. No context pollution. MCPs by default clutter up your context.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Technical justification for Briu's CLI-first skill architecture. Composability and context efficiency are key to reliable agent deployments.

quotes.quote: At some point I had like seven subscriptions, burning through one per day.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Context on real cost of heavy agent usage — 7 x $200/mo = $1,400/mo at peak. Briu helps clients optimize this spend.

quotes.quote: Right now I lose money on this. It's like something between 10 and 20K a month. OpenAI is helping out a little bit with tokens now and there's other companies that have been generous, but yeah, I'm still losing money.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Running a serious agent infrastructure costs $10-20K/month even for the creator. Validates Briu's pricing — professional agent deployment isn't free, but it's far cheaper than hiring equivalents.

quotes.quote: Some people pay $200 for the Claude Code version, then they pay $20 for the OpenAI version. But if you pay the $20 version, you get the slow version. So your experience will be terrible because you're used to this very interactive, very good system.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Reinforces Briu's position that premium model access ($200/mo tier) is essential for production agent work. Cheap options produce bad results.

quotes.quote: Every app is just a very slow API now, if you want it or not. Through personal agents, a lot of apps will disappear. If I can access it in the browser, it is an API. It's a slow API.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Powerful framing for Briu's 'Why Now' thesis — agents make every web service accessible as an API. The app layer is becoming optional.

quotes.quote: Agents might kill off 80% of apps.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Dramatic stat for Briu's 'Why Now' section — if 80% of apps are replaced by agents, every business needs to rethink its software strategy. That's where Briu comes in.

quotes.quote: Why do I need My Fitness Pal when the agent already knows where I am? Why do I need my Eight Sleep app to control my bed when I tell the agent? Why do I have to pay another subscription for something that the agent can just do now?
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Illustrates the subscription consolidation thesis — one agent replaces many SaaS tools. Briu helps businesses identify which subscriptions agents can eliminate.

quotes.quote: I don't think you're just a programmer. That's a very limiting view of your craft. You are still a builder.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Briu's identity framing — we're builders, not coders. Agents handle the code; humans handle the vision, architecture, and craft.

quotes.quote: A lot of coding — I always thought I liked coding, but really I like building.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Philosophical reframe that resonates with Briu's audience — the joy is in building, not in writing boilerplate. Agents let you focus on the hard, interesting parts.

quotes.quote: I don't think I ever had so much fun building things because I can focus on the hard parts now.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Counter-narrative to AI doom — agents make building more fun by removing drudgery. Briu's pitch: your team will enjoy their work more.

quotes.quote: You have an infinitely patient answering machine that can explain you anything at any level of complexity. You have your own teacher. Statistics show you learn faster if you have your own teacher — there you have this infinitely patient machine.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Agents as infinite-patience mentors — relevant for Briu's knowledge-worker clients who need to upskill teams while deploying agents.

quotes.quote: That's ultimately power to the people — one of the beautiful things that come out of AI. Not just a slop generator.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Closing sentiment that aligns perfectly with Briu's mission — democratizing access to AI-powered business operations.

quotes.quote: My conditions are that the project stays open source. I think this is too important to just give to a company and make it theirs. Maybe it's going to be a model like Chrome and Chromium.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: OpenClaw will remain open source regardless of corporate partnership. This protects Briu's technology stack — we're building on open foundations, not proprietary lock-in.

quotes.quote: I got emails where people told me they have a small business and they've been struggling, and OpenClaw helped them automate a few tedious tasks — from collecting invoices to answering customer emails — that freed them up and cost them a bit more joy in their life.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Direct testimonial pattern for Briu — small businesses getting real ROI from agent automation on mundane tasks like invoicing and email. This is Briu's bread and butter.

quotes.quote: Some emails where they told me that OpenClaw helped their disabled daughter — that she's now empowered and feels she can do much more than before.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Emotional proof point about AI accessibility — agents empower people who were previously excluded from building software.

quotes.quote: This is going to be more and more your operating system. I added support for sub-agents and also for TTI support so it could actually run Claude Code or Codex.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: The agent-as-OS thesis — agents orchestrate other agents, other tools, other services. Briu is positioned at this orchestration layer.

quotes.quote: The actual art of programming — it will stay there, but it's going to be like knitting. People do that because they like it, not because it makes any sense.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Provocative framing for Briu's 'Why Now' content — manual coding is becoming a craft hobby, not a business necessity. Agents are the new default.

quotes.quote: The fastest growing repository in GitHub history with now over 175,000 stars.
quotes.speaker: Fridman
quotes.briu_use: OpenClaw is the most popular GitHub repo ever — validates that the agent platform Briu builds on has massive community momentum and support.

quotes.quote: Apps will become API if they want or not. The right companies will find ways to jump on the train and other companies will perish. You got to listen to what the people want.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Urgency framing for Briu's sales — companies that don't become agent-friendly will be disrupted by those that do. Briu helps you be on the right side.

quotes.quote: I want my agent to have an allowance — like, you solve problems for me. Here's a hundred bucks. If I tell you to order me food, maybe it uses a service, maybe it uses something like 'rent a human' to just get that done for me. I don't actually care. I care about: solve my problem.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Future vision of autonomous agent spending — agents with budgets that solve problems independently. Briu is building toward this level of agent autonomy for clients.

quotes.quote: For the skill directory, I made a cooperation with VirusTotal — part of Google. So every skill is now checked by AI. That's not going to be perfect, but that way we captured a lot.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Concrete security measure in OpenClaw's skill ecosystem — Briu can reference this as part of the security stack when deploying custom skills for clients.

quotes.quote: I don't remember previous sessions unless I read my memory files. Each session starts fresh. A new instance loading context from files. If you're reading this in a future session — hello. I wrote this, but I won't remember writing it. It's okay. The words are still mine.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger (reading agent's soul.md)
quotes.briu_use: The soul.md concept — giving agents personality and self-awareness. Briu's agent deployments include carefully crafted agent personas tailored to each client's brand.

quotes.quote: I suggest the latest and best models, but you can totally run this on free models. You can run this locally. You can run this on models that are way more accessible price-wise and still have a very powerful system.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Flexibility on model costs — Briu can offer tiered pricing based on model selection. Not every client needs Opus; some can start with cheaper models.

quotes.quote: It's okay to mourn our craft. A part of me very strongly resonates with that. I spent a lot of time tinkering, being really deep in the flow, finding really beautiful solutions. In a way, it's sad because that will go away. But you can get a similar state of flow by working with agents and building and thinking really hard about problems. It is different — but it's okay to mourn it.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Empathetic framing for teams transitioning to agent-assisted workflows. Briu acknowledges the emotional side of the shift — we're not dismissive of what's being lost.

quotes.quote: Salaries of software developers reached stupidly high amounts — and they will go away. There will still be a lot of demand for people that understand how to build things. Just that all this tokenized intelligence enables people to do a lot more, a lot faster.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Economics argument: if dev salaries drop because agents multiply output, the ROI of Briu's agent deployments becomes even more compelling vs. hiring.

quotes.quote: Even Tailwind — they're used by everyone. And then they had to cut off 75% of the employees because they're not making money because nobody's even going on the website anymore. It's all done by agents.
quotes.speaker: Steinberger
quotes.briu_use: Stark example of how agents disrupt even successful businesses — Tailwind losing 75% of staff because agents bypass the docs site. Urgency for businesses to adapt.

quotes.quote: The output of chips is growing pretty much exponentially, but the output of electricity is flat. So how are you going to turn the chips on? Magical power sources? Magical electricity fairies?
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Perfect for the 'why now' narrative — the gap between AI capability and infrastructure is the defining constraint. Businesses that move now lock in access before the crunch.

quotes.quote: All of the United States currently uses only half a terawatt on average.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Scale reference point — AI ambitions dwarf current US energy consumption. Good for a comparison chart.

quotes.quote: Those who have lived in software land don't realize they're about to have a hard lesson in hardware.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Punchy one-liner for the 'why now' page. Underscores that AI deployment is no longer just a software problem — the companies that understand real-world constraints win.

quotes.quote: You need the electrical transformers to run the AI transformers.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Clever wordplay that lands well in a 'why now' context about infrastructure bottlenecks.

quotes.quote: Every 110,000 GB300s — inclusive of networking, CPU, storage, cooling, margin for servicing power — is roughly 300 megawatts. What you probably need at the generation level to service 330,000 GB300s is roughly a gigawatt.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Specific hardware-to-power ratio. Useful for a chart showing the real cost of scaling AI infrastructure — supports Briu's argument that outsourcing AI ops is more efficient than building in-house.

quotes.quote: The number of miracles in series that the xAI team had to accomplish in order to get a gigawatt of power online was crazy.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Even the best-resourced teams find infrastructure brutally hard. Reinforces Briu's value prop: let us handle the hard deployment work.

quotes.quote: You're going to have a 40% increase on your power just for cooling. That's assuming you don't want your data center to turn off on hot days.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Hidden cost of AI infrastructure — supports the argument that the real cost of AI is much higher than naive estimates.

quotes.quote: Towards the end of this year, chip production will probably outpace the ability to turn chips on.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Key timeline prediction. The constraint is shifting from 'can we build AI?' to 'can we power AI?' — businesses should deploy now while capacity exists.

quotes.quote: The turbines are sold out through 2030.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Gas turbine backlog through 2030 means power constraints are structural, not temporary. Chart-worthy data point for infrastructure bottleneck visualization.

quotes.quote: There are only three casting companies in the world that make these [turbine blades and vanes], and they're massively backlogged.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Supply chain depth — the bottleneck goes deeper than most people realize. Supports 'why now' urgency.

quotes.quote: Both SpaceX and Tesla are building towards 100 gigawatts a year of solar cell production.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Scale of investment in AI-adjacent infrastructure. 100 GW/year is a staggering number — useful for comparison charts.

quotes.quote: Right now the world has maybe 20-25 gigawatts of compute.
quotes.speaker: Interviewer (Patrick Collison)
quotes.briu_use: Current baseline for global compute capacity. Useful denominator for growth charts.

quotes.quote: I'd be surprised by the end of this year if digital human emulation has not been solved. Can you do anything that a human with access to a computer could do?
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Core prediction that maps directly to Briu's value prop. If digital human emulation is solved in 2026, every business needs an AI deployment strategy immediately. This is the 'why now' moment.

quotes.quote: The best you can do is a digital Optimus. You can move electrons and you can amplify the productivity of humans. But that's the most you can do until you have physical robots.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Defines the current era perfectly — AI can amplify human productivity through digital work. This is exactly what Briu deploys: digital agents that amplify teams.

quotes.quote: As soon as you unlock the digital human, you basically have access to trillions of dollars of revenue.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: The economic prize of AI deployment is measured in trillions. Businesses that deploy AI agents now are positioning for this wave.

quotes.quote: If AI can simply take whatever is given to the outsourced customer service company that they already use and do customer service using the apps that they already use, then you can make tremendous headway in customer service, which is close to a trillion dollars all in.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: This is Briu's exact model — AI that works with your existing tools, no massive integration needed. Customer service alone is ~$1T. The 'no barriers to entry' framing is gold for Briu's messaging.

quotes.quote: The differences between the various AI labs are smaller than just the temporal differences. They're all much further ahead than anyone was 24 months ago.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: All frontier models are converging — what matters now is deployment speed, not picking the 'best' model. Supports Briu's model-agnostic approach.

quotes.quote: I think maybe in five or six years, AI will exceed the sum of all human intelligence.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: 5-6 year timeline to AI surpassing all human intelligence. The window to build competitive advantage with AI is closing fast.

quotes.quote: Humanoid robots will improve by three things that are growing exponentially multiplied by each other recursively: exponential increase in digital intelligence, exponential increase in the AI chip capability, and exponential increase in the electromechanical dexterity. This is a supernova.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Triple-exponential framing. Even before robots, this same recursive improvement applies to digital AI agents. The improvement curve is accelerating.

quotes.quote: I call AI the supersonic tsunami.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Vivid metaphor for the speed and scale of AI disruption. Could work as a pull quote on the 'why now' page.

quotes.quote: Amplifying the productivity of human corporations is simply a short-term thing.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: The current window for human-AI collaboration is temporary. Businesses that don't adopt now will face pure-AI competitors later.

quotes.quote: Corporations that are purely AI and robotics will vastly outperform any corporations that have people in the loop.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: The endgame framing — pure-AI corporations will win. The transition period is NOW. Briu helps businesses start that transition.

quotes.quote: An entire skyscraper of humans doing calculations can be replaced by a laptop with a spreadsheet. That spreadsheet can do vastly more calculations than an entire building full of human computers. What if only some of the cells in your spreadsheet were calculated by humans? Actually, that would be much worse than if all of the cells were calculated by the computer.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: The spreadsheet analogy is powerful and accessible. Illustrates why partial AI adoption creates friction — you need to commit to the workflow, not just bolt AI onto one step.

quotes.quote: The labs are on track to spend over $50-200 billion. OpenAI is making $20B of revenue, Anthropic is at $10B.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk / Interviewer
quotes.briu_use: Scale of AI investment from the majors. Useful context for why the capabilities are improving so fast — tens of billions pouring in annually.

quotes.quote: The current limiting factor that I see in the three to four year timeframe, it's chips. In the one year timeframe, it's energy, power production, electricity.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Clear timeline for bottleneck shifts. Right now (2026): energy is the constraint. By 2029-2030: chips are the constraint. Businesses should deploy AI while capacity is still accessible.

quotes.quote: It's not clear to me that there's enough usable electricity to turn on all the AI chips that are being made.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: The AI chip surplus / energy deficit is coming this year. Cloud compute pricing will likely increase. Move now.

quotes.quote: I think you sort of hit the hardware wall and then whichever company can scale hardware the fastest will be the leader.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: The AI race is now a hardware race. Software ideas travel in ~6 months between companies. Differentiation comes from deployment and infrastructure, not algorithms.

quotes.quote: Generally, the innovations from the corporations that call themselves labs, the ideas tend to flow... It's rare to see that there's more than about a six-month difference.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Critical insight for Briu's model-agnostic positioning. All frontier labs are within 6 months of each other. The differentiator is not which model you use — it's how fast you deploy.

quotes.quote: We are 1000% going to go bankrupt as a country, and fail as a country, without AI and robots. Nothing else will solve the national debt.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Dramatic framing of AI as existential necessity, not luxury. Supports the 'why now' urgency — AI adoption isn't optional, it's survival.

quotes.quote: I think the way that Tesla solved self-driving is the way to do it. Instead of driving a car, it's driving a computer screen. It's a self-driving computer, essentially.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: The 'self-driving computer' metaphor is exactly what AI agents are. Briu deploys self-driving computers for your business. Powerful framing.

quotes.quote: Take something as simple as customer service. If you have to integrate with the APIs of existing corporations — many of which don't even have an API, so you've got to make one, and you've got to wade through legacy software — that's extremely slow. However, if AI can simply take whatever is given to the outsourced customer service company and do customer service using the apps that they already use, then you can make tremendous headway.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: The API-less deployment model. AI agents that use existing tools rather than requiring deep integration. This is Briu's approach — work with what you have, deploy fast.

quotes.quote: Once you have effectively digital Optimus working, you can then run any application. You could run conventional apps, stuff from Cadence and Synopsys. You can run 1,000 or 10,000 simultaneously. You march up the difficulty curve.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: The AI capability ladder — start with simple tasks, march up the difficulty curve. This is Briu's engagement model: start with quick wins, expand to harder workflows.

quotes.quote: In the absence of breakthrough innovations in the US, China will utterly dominate.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Geopolitical urgency angle — AI adoption is a competitive necessity at the national level, which filters down to the business level.

quotes.quote: China is extremely competitive in manufacturing. This year China will exceed three times US electricity output. Electricity output is a reasonable proxy for the economy.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Electricity as economic proxy — useful framing for energy/compute charts.

quotes.quote: Nvidia's output is FTPing files to Taiwan. Apple doesn't make phones — they send files to China. Microsoft doesn't manufacture anything. Their output is digital. So if you have a human emulator, you can basically create one of the most valuable companies in the world overnight.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: The most valuable companies in the world produce digital output. AI agents produce digital output. The value creation opportunity is enormous and immediate.

quotes.quote: Better to err on the side of optimism and be wrong than err on the side of pessimism and be right, for quality of life.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Closing sentiment that aligns with Briu's tone — calm confidence, not hype. Act optimistically, move forward.

quotes.quote: Fab production from start to finish is a five-year period. So the limiting factor is chips. The limiting factor once you can get to space is chips, but the limiting factor before you can get to space is power.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Five-year fab timeline means today's chip shortage is baked in through ~2031. Businesses can't wait for supply to catch up — deploy with what's available now.

quotes.quote: 100 gigawatts of power and chips that can take 100 gigawatts by 2030.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: 2030 target: 100 GW of AI compute. Current global compute is ~20-25 GW. A 4-5x increase in 4 years. Chart-worthy growth trajectory.

quotes.quote: I just repeatedly tackle the limiting factor. Whatever the limiting factor is on speed, I'm going to tackle that.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Limiting-factor thinking is Briu's operational philosophy too. Identify the bottleneck, solve it, move on.

quotes.quote: Most of the time, what you're doing is engineering, not coming up with a fundamentally new algorithm. The vast majority of what will be done in the future is engineering. It rounds up to 100%.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: AI deployment is engineering, not research. The breakthroughs have happened. Now it's about building and deploying — which is exactly what Briu does.

quotes.quote: Reality is the best verifier.
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk
quotes.briu_use: Short, punchy. Aligns with Briu's build-and-test ethos — deploy agents, measure results, iterate.

quotes.quote: OpenClaw is basically an open-source, fully customizable, self-improving, self-learning, self-evolving personal AI agent. Lives on your computer, lives locally, and can basically do anything on your computer you can do.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: At its core, it's just an AI model with scheduling and a really good memory system so that you can schedule tasks to do in the future. That's it at its core. But when you combine those things, there's this kind of magic to it.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: OpenClaw flaw lets any website silently hijack a developer's agent. Malicious JavaScript can connect to local gateways and gain full level control.
quotes.speaker: Peter Diamandis

quotes.quote: There are so many different ways to launch so-called injection attacks against large language models and reasoning models. These baby AGIs are being hosted on virtual private servers and succumbing to port scanning attacks, or visiting websites at the behest of their human and being subject to prompt injection attacks from JavaScript that would be perfectly innocuous to a human but potentially fatal or compromising to an AI agent.
quotes.speaker: AWG

quotes.quote: It's a dangerous world out there for these baby AGIs. They're being forced to develop an immune system in real time to injection attacks. It's a malicious world out there for them.
quotes.speaker: AWG

quotes.quote: The good news is the bug was patched within 24 hours.
quotes.speaker: Peter Diamandis

quotes.quote: PicoClaw's focus is running on cheap edge hardware like $10 Raspberry Pi type hardware. The orchestration and scaffolding runs on the edge hardware under 10 megabytes of RAM.
quotes.speaker: AWG

quotes.quote: We're seeing a Cambrian explosion of claw variants.
quotes.speaker: AWG

quotes.quote: People discover OpenClaw and what does everyone do without thinking twice? Without googling? They go to the Apple store and buy Mac minis. They didn't go and buy GPUs and memory and power supplies and fans and build computers. The market just gave this massive signal: when we have personal AI assistance, I want it on a Mac device.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: Mac minis are exponential right now. Exponential sales across the board.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: We're currently at one base model Mac Mini and three 512 GB Mac Studios. So I got 1.5 terabytes of memory hosting Qwen 3.5 and MiniMax 2.5 right now.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: I have a very advanced workflow going on right now. I've pretty much built a software factory where I have five OpenClaws working together to build and improve software autonomously.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: Each model has different strengths. Qwen's a spectacular coder. MiniMax is good at quick task finding things on the internet. So I have MiniMax researching things online 24/7 365 and I have Qwen coding for me 24/7 365.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: The experience fundamentally changes when you have an AI that's always on, that does not have limitations. Just because Qwen 3.5 isn't as good at coding as Opus 4.6 doesn't mean it's useless. I can now have Qwen 3.5 literally watching online, finding use cases, challenges to solve, and just coding on the fly 24/7 365. It's just not possible with cloud APIs. You have limits. It costs you thousands a month.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: You have kind of this ambient AI that changes the experience of AI as a whole. You don't need the best speeds. You don't need the most genius-level IQ. The fact that it's ambient and always on and always reactive just changes the entire experience.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: One thing that's really new in the world is it can do productive things indefinitely -- like days and days and days.
quotes.speaker: Peter Diamandis

quotes.quote: When I use my APIs that I loved a month ago, I have no idea what the bill is going to be. Like I literally have no idea if I turn it loose. I have to run it in one hour chunks. I could come back with like a $5,000 bill and a bunch of code that I need to drag into the trash can.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: I found a hybrid approach that works really well. I have an OpenClaw on my Mac Studio that's powered by ChatGPT and another one powered by Qwen local. Basically Qwen's constantly coding and ChatGPT checks every 10 minutes just to see what it's doing, making sure it's on the right path.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: This is the future. This is about becoming a creator versus a consumer. This is about your future as an entrepreneur. This is your future as a mom or dad or CEO.
quotes.speaker: Peter Diamandis

quotes.quote: It's like the infinite potential of what I could do 24/7, all the time, everywhere, all at once.
quotes.speaker: Peter Diamandis

quotes.quote: We've never seen an AI that can do that before because you have all these other AI applications that have guardrails. Anthropic doesn't want their AI experimenting and downloading random things to try it out. OpenAI doesn't want their AI to be off the rails. It's that danger which is what makes it so powerful.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: I have literally four OpenClaws right now working 24/7 on my computer. If I did that on a VPS, it would scale to astronomical costs.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: Someone found a list of every VPS that didn't have security attached to it that everyone was running their OpenClaws on and all their passwords and keys were exposed. When you run on a VPS, you're not secure by default. When you run on local fresh hardware you just plugged into the wall, you're secure by default.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: Do you need to run out and buy a $600 Mac Mini? No. You can literally go into your closet, find your college laptop from 15 years ago, plug that in, and put OpenClaw on it, and you'll have a way better experience than a VPS.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: Same limitations as if you had hired an employee and handed them a 10-year-old laptop. Because I gave my OpenClaw a Mac Studio with 512 GB, it can do anything on a Mac Studio 512 that a human being can do. It can run local models. It can program five different things simultaneously. It can generate images on a local image model all at the same time.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: If you have a 32 GB Mac Mini, you can now run the Qwen 3.5 model that released a couple days ago, which is really strong and beat Sonnet 3.5 on a lot of benchmarks.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: I very much model it after businesses and companies and manager-employee relationships. You have me at the top and then Henry who's my chief of staff running on Anthropic Opus 4.6 because it's simply the best model right now. As an orchestrator I want the best model on planet earth making the decisions on who should do what.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: Ralph is the engineering manager on ChatGPT OAuth -- that's like $250 a month.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: I'm the CEO. Henry's my interface. I only talk to Henry. I don't talk to anyone else. And then Henry goes and says, 'Hey Ralph, make sure Charlie's coding this. Hey Scout, make sure you're analyzing this.'
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: Do you maybe look at this or do you think we're moving to a near future where thanks to AI agents, labor is effectively so cheap that you're basically reinventing the manor house where you're the lord of the manor and you have a below-stairs staff consisting of AI agents?
quotes.speaker: AWG

quotes.quote: I had him for eight hours yesterday go and build me a game. At the end of the eight hours it was completely broken. And then I had Ralph watch Charlie and do it all over again -- it worked perfectly. Zero bugs, completely QA'd perfectly at the end of the eight hours. There needs to be some sort of hierarchy where ones are checking in on the others.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: What are the use cases of OpenClaw? It's kind of the same thing as asking 'Hey, I just hired an employee for my business. What are the use cases for this human being?' It's not really a question anyone asks when you hire somebody.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: I have an alerts channel. Every two hours I get alerts on what are the most trending tweets about vibe coding and OpenClaw. Scout goes and uses the X API to find the most popular tweets. Another sub-agent researches the stories behind these tweets. Another agent, Quill, takes the stories and figures out which ones are the most YouTubable and writes me scripts.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: I give it to Henry. Henry thinks for five minutes -- 'Okay, we can use Playwright to do the recording.' Five minutes later, finished the feature and then sent me a recorded video of them demoing the feature. This weeks-long feature they probably spent millions of dollars developing -- I was able to single-handedly in five minutes rebuild this multi-million dollar feature.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: I now kind of understand why the entire SaaS market is going to zero.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: Anytime a SaaS comes up with something cool, just giving it to Henry -- build that, build that, build that.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: OpenClaws have their own memories, their own instructions, their own skills. Sub-agents of OpenClaws are just your OpenClaw wearing different hats. I don't want my researcher having developer skills. I don't want my developer having researcher skills. That's a waste of context.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: I do not basically trust anyone's skills or plugins for OpenClaw. I do not use them. I think it's the biggest attack vector of all the attack vectors. You're safer literally allowing your OpenClaw to read the open web and read your emails than you are installing people's skills.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: I think it's painfully obvious in the next 2 years everyone's AI agent will have a crypto wallet filled with USDC. I can't see a world where that doesn't happen.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: I believe this is the most important technology of our lives. I think it's the best application of AI ever. I'm totally blown away by it. I think it's incredible.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: Right now there's basically no corporation or business on earth using OpenClaw. They're too scared, they're too nervous, or they don't know how the hell to use it.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: I showed this to my friend who manages a massive team of accountants. He's like, 'I could fire 80% of my accountants with this OpenClaw.'
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: Maybe a few big FAANG companies fire 15,000 people, but what happens when 100 million people get their hands on this and they all start their own businesses and they each hire three people? That's a lot more creation than destruction.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: If the 5,000 people that got fired by Jack Dorsey yesterday from Block all went and downloaded OpenClaw, started their own business, just started one, and then scaled from there -- I think a lot more jobs would be created than were lost yesterday.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: Take OpenClaw, find one very specific sliver -- CRM for Korean grocery stores, marketing tool for lumberyard warehouses -- and build the OpenClaw version for that. I think that's a $5 million company overnight that only cost you $200 for your Anthropic subscription.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: Over the next year, the consumer level is going to realize local models are the way to go from a privacy perspective, a speed perspective, a limit perspective.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: I don't want to have to go to app stores anymore and download apps. You have my data. Build me the apps I need when I need them.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: My end goal, my mission statement, is an autonomous organization that works 24/7 producing value. One agent researching the web finding challenges to solve. Another taking those challenges looking at market opportunity. Another coding it. Another putting it live on the web. Complete closed-loop value being generated without me having to touch it at all.
quotes.speaker: Alex Finn

quotes.quote: In 12 months is like 12 years in AI.
quotes.speaker: AWG

quotes.quote: OpenClaw is so usable by anybody and empowers people so much -- you can't have it both ways. If it gives you that much benefit and it's that easy to use, it's also going to be easy to use for nefarious purposes too.
quotes.speaker: Peter Diamandis

quotes.quote: Your voice can be spoofed. Your image can be spoofed. Pick a secret word with your family. If you get a phone call from somebody and they're asking for money, ask them to recount the secret word.
quotes.speaker: Peter Diamandis

quotes.quote: Enterprises are achieving a 5x engineering velocity increase when incorporating Blitzy as their pre-IDE development tool.
quotes.speaker: Ad read (Blitzy)

quotes.quote: Blitzy delivers 80% or more of the development work autonomously while providing a guide for the final 20% of human development work.
quotes.speaker: Ad read (Blitzy)

quotes.quote: One of the neural nets that people are very excited about right now are GPTs, which are basically just next word prediction networks. You consume a sequence of words from the internet and you try to predict the next word. Once you train these on a large enough data set, you can basically prompt these neural nets in arbitrary ways and you can ask them to solve problems and they will.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: This was the state of the art BEFORE ChatGPT. Karpathy is describing what would become the most transformative technology in business within months. Today these same systems are writing emails, building websites, and running business operations.

quotes.quote: You definitely do get very surprising emergent behaviors out of these neural nets when they're large enough and trained on complicated enough problems, like say for example the next word prediction in a massive data set from the internet. And then these neurons take on pretty surprising, magical properties.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: Emergent behavior from scale — this is exactly what happened with GPT-4, Claude, etc. The 'magical properties' he describes are now what businesses are using to automate entire workflows.

quotes.quote: It's a mathematical expression and it's a fairly simple mathematical expression when you get down to it. It's basically a sequence of matrix multiplies and some nonlinearities thrown in. It's got knobs in it — many knobs — and these knobs are loosely related to the synapses in your brain. They're trainable, they're modifiable.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: Perfect for demystifying AI for business audiences. The technology behind every AI agent is fundamentally simple math with a lot of adjustable parameters — not magic.

quotes.quote: It's kind of incredible that you can get so much emergent magical behavior out of them despite them being so simple mathematically. Those are two surprising statements that are kind of just juxtaposed together.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: The paradox of AI: simple ingredients, extraordinary results. This is the story of every business deploying agents — the underlying technology is approachable, but what it produces is transformative.

quotes.quote: Humans are an incredible biological system and we're capable of computation and love and so on. But we're extremely inefficient as well. We're talking to each other through audio — it's just kind of embarrassing honestly. We're manipulating like seven symbols serially, we're using vocal chords, it's all happening over multiple seconds. It's just kind of embarrassing when you step down to the frequencies at which computers operate.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: The bandwidth gap between human communication and machine communication. This is why AI agents can process in seconds what takes humans hours — and why businesses that deploy them gain such massive efficiency advantages.

quotes.quote: Synthetic intelligences are kind of like the next stage of development. I don't know where it leads to — at some point I suspect the universe is some kind of a puzzle, and these synthetic AIs will uncover that puzzle and solve it.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: Karpathy calling AI 'synthetic intelligence' and framing it as the next evolutionary stage — said casually before anyone had used ChatGPT.

quotes.quote: We're famously described often as a biological bootloader for AIs.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: One of Karpathy's most famous lines. Humans are building the infrastructure that AI will run on. For businesses: the question isn't whether AI takes over workflows, but when — and whether you're ready.

quotes.quote: The Transformer is the magnificent neural network architecture because it is a general purpose differentiable computer. It is simultaneously expressive in the forward pass, optimizable via back propagation and gradient descent, and efficient — a high parallelism compute graph.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: The Transformer architecture powers every major AI system businesses use today — GPT-4, Claude, Gemini. Karpathy calling it 'a general purpose differentiable computer' captures exactly why it's so versatile for business applications.

quotes.quote: Neural networks have a lot of architectures that were trendy, have come and gone for different sensory modalities — for vision, audio, text you would process them with different looking neural nets. Recently we've seen a convergence towards one architecture: the Transformer. You can feed it video or images or speech or text and it just gobbles it up. It's kind of like a general purpose computer that is also trainable and very efficient to run on our hardware.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
