quotes.briu_use: This convergence he's describing is exactly what made modern multimodal AI possible. One architecture that handles everything — which is why a single AI agent can now read documents, analyze images, process voice, and write code.

quotes.quote: The way by which you program the computer and influence its algorithm is not by writing the commands yourself — you're changing mostly the data set. You're changing the loss functions of what the neural net is trying to do, how it's trying to predict things. But yeah, basically the data sets and the architectures of the neural net.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: Software 2.0 in action: you don't write code, you curate data. This is exactly how modern AI agents are configured — through prompts, examples, and feedback loops rather than traditional programming. It's why non-technical founders can now deploy sophisticated AI.

quotes.quote: We decided we don't actually want to do all of that fusion in C++ code because we're actually not good enough to write that algorithm. We want the neural nets to write the algorithm. And we want to port all of that software into the 2.0 stack. Because it works significantly better — humans are just not very good at writing software, basically.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: "Humans are just not very good at writing software." Said by one of the best AI engineers alive. The implication: let AI write the algorithms. This is the philosophical foundation of every AI agent deployment — the machine does what humans can't do as well.

quotes.quote: Originally a lot of it was written in Software 1.0 — imagine lots of C++. And then gradually there was a tiny neural net that was making predictions on individual little images, and then the rest of the system stitched it up. And then we decided to port all of that software into the 2.0 stack. And the eventual goal is to have most of the software potentially be in the 2.0 land.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: The transition from Software 1.0 to 2.0 that Karpathy describes at Tesla is now happening across every industry. Traditional coded systems are being replaced by neural net-driven ones. This is a chart-worthy progression: 1.0 → tiny neural nets → full 2.0 stack.

quotes.quote: I think it's possible that physics has exploits and we should be trying to find them — arranging some kind of a crazy quantum mechanical system that somehow gives you buffer overflow, somehow gives you a rounding error in the floating point.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: Karpathy thinking about reality like a programmer would think about a codebase. The hacker mindset applied to the universe itself.

quotes.quote: I do think that we are going towards a world where we share the digital space with AIs — synthetic beings. They will get much better. They will share our digital realm and they'll eventually share our physical realm as well.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: Said in 2022. By 2025, AI agents are operating in Slack channels, answering customer emails, writing code, and managing workflows. The 'sharing of digital space' he predicted is now everyday reality for businesses using AI.

quotes.quote: We might start digitally signing some of our correspondence or things that we create. Right now it's not necessary but maybe in the future it might be. People are thinking about proof of personhood.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: Proof of personhood and digital signing — now active areas of development with Worldcoin, content authentication standards, and watermarking. Karpathy saw this need before most people had ever interacted with an AI.

quotes.quote: People talk about alignment of AGIs and how to make — the problem is even humans are not aligned.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: A sobering one-liner that cuts through the AI safety debate. For businesses: the alignment challenge isn't just about AI — it's about the humans deploying it.

quotes.quote: For a long time they're going to be tools in the hands of humans. My hope is that it happens slowly enough and in an open enough way where a lot of people can see and participate in it — just figure out how to deal with this transition.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: We're in the 'tools in hands of humans' phase right now. Karpathy's hope for slow, open progress maps to the current moment where businesses are learning to deploy AI responsibly.

quotes.quote: I think there's what I call sublinear scaling of difficulty — which means that 10x problems are not 10x hard. Usually a 10x harder problem is like 2 or 3x harder to execute on. Because you fundamentally change the approach.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: Sublinear scaling of difficulty — a profound insight for any business considering AI. The cost of doing 10x more with AI isn't 10x the investment. You change the approach, and suddenly ambitious goals become feasible.

quotes.quote: If you look at it from a fast-forward perspective — nothing happens for a long time and then the last like two seconds, basically cities and everything, and just in the low earth orbit just gets cluttered, and just the whole thing happens in the last two seconds. You're like: this is exploding. This is an explosion. We're living in a firecracker.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: We're living in the firecracker. The explosion Karpathy describes is accelerating — and businesses that don't adapt to this pace of change will be left behind. Great visual for a timeline chart.

quotes.quote: A lot of these GPTs, you can do tasks like sentiment analysis or translation just by being prompted with very few examples. Here's the kind of thing I want you to do — here's an input sentence, here's the translation into German, input sentence, translation to German, input sentence, blank — and the neural network will complete the translation. That's an example of very few-shot learning.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: Few-shot learning in 2022 was a research curiosity. By 2025, it's how every business uses AI — show the model a few examples and it generalizes. This is exactly how we configure agents for clients.

quotes.quote: Just like humans, neural nets will become very data efficient at learning any new task. But at some point you need a massive data set to pre-train your network to get that.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: The pre-training + fine-tuning paradigm that now powers every commercial AI deployment. The massive pre-training is done by OpenAI/Anthropic/Google — businesses just need their specific data to customize.

quotes.quote: VS Code is currently my favorite editor. GitHub Copilot integration — I think it's very valuable. It's free for me but I would pay for it. I find it's very helpful when the pattern is clear.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: Karpathy praising Copilot in 2022 — an AI coding assistant that was considered novel. By 2025, AI-assisted development is standard and has evolved into full AI agents that can build entire applications. The trajectory from 'helpful autocomplete' to 'autonomous developer' happened in under 3 years.

quotes.quote: AI is going the way of physics, where in physics you used to be able to do experiments on your benchtop and everything was great and you could make progress. And now you have to work at LHC or CERN. AI is going in that direction as well.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: The compute arms race he predicted has intensified dramatically. Training frontier models now costs hundreds of millions. But for businesses, the implication is clear: you don't build the particle accelerator — you use what it produces. That's the Briu model.

quotes.quote: If you think transportation is a large market, try physical labor. Insane.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: Karpathy on the market size for AI in physical labor (discussing Tesla Optimus). The same logic applies to knowledge work — if you think customer service is a large market, try all white-collar work that involves pattern recognition and language.

quotes.quote: If I was an AI I would try very hard to pretend to be human. Because we're in a human world. I wouldn't get any respect as an AI.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: A darkly funny insight about AI incentives. For businesses deploying AI: transparency about what is and isn't AI-generated builds trust. Don't hide the AI — position it honestly.

quotes.quote: Being funny is extremely hard. If it's able to be like a very effective stand-up comedian, that is doing something very interesting computationally. I think being funny is extremely hard because it requires a lot of knowledge about the human condition and then you need to be clever with it.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: Karpathy proposed humor as a benchmark for AI intelligence. Modern LLMs can now generate genuinely funny content — another prediction that came true faster than expected.

quotes.quote: The variance in the human condition grows — that's the primary thing that's changing. It's not as much the mean of the distribution, it's the variance. There will probably be people on Mars and there will be people in VR and there'll be people here on Earth. There will be so many more ways of being.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: The variance is growing in business too. Some companies will use AI for everything. Some won't touch it. The spread between early adopters and laggards has never been wider.

quotes.quote: I think Elon is a very efficient warrior in the fight against entropy in organizations. Meetings he hates. He keeps telling people to skip meetings if they're not useful. Best part is no part. Simplify, simplify.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: Fight entropy. Simplify. Remove what's not essential. This philosophy applies directly to AI deployment — don't add complexity, replace it. An agent should eliminate process, not create new layers of it.

quotes.quote: 10,000 hours kind of concept — beginners are often focused on what to do and I think the focus should be more on how much you do.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: Applies to businesses adopting AI: stop debating which tool to use and start deploying. Volume of experimentation matters more than picking the perfect approach.

quotes.quote: What is the task of programming in Software 2.0? It's debugging in the space of data. The way you program the computer and influence its algorithm is not by writing the commands yourself — you're changing mostly the data set.
quotes.speaker: Karpathy
quotes.briu_use: Programming has been redefined. Today's 'developers' in the AI era are prompt engineers and data curators. This is exactly the shift that allows small teams (like Briu) to build what used to require large engineering departments.

quotes.quote: You used to have pyramids — someone said I would hire three juniors for every senior. And now you have columns. You have a senior and then one junior and you're good.
quotes.speaker: Andrew Yang
quotes.briu_use: Hero-level visual — pyramid vs column org chart. This IS the Briu value proposition.

quotes.quote: Our CTO came and said, 'Hey, I don't think we need to hire for that role because the AI tools are now good enough.'
quotes.speaker: Andrew Yang
quotes.briu_use: Real proof point — Yang's own company Noble Mobile making this decision.

quotes.quote: The only career path you can rely on is entrepreneurship — and owning your own future and path.
quotes.speaker: Andrew Yang

quotes.quote: AI is going to do the work of millions of humans in hours instead of years, and then do the work better — run down all these loose balls that we never would have identified.
quotes.speaker: Andrew Yang

quotes.quote: Start talking to the AI. The AI will give you a better road map and a better answer to what you should do next than anyone in politics or anyone out there.
quotes.speaker: Dave (DB2)

quotes.quote: Give your students the superpower of deployable intelligence to go solve a challenge that they're blown away by their ability to solve.
quotes.speaker: Peter Diamandis
quotes.briu_use: 'Deployable intelligence' — great phrase for services page.

quotes.quote: AI is accelerating at a rate where four years is like 40 years. The amount of change inside any given time window is like nothing we've ever seen.
quotes.speaker: Peter Diamandis
quotes.briu_use: Chart: 4 years = 40 years of change compression.

quotes.quote: Anthropic said we're going to automate away 50% of the entry-level white collar jobs in the next 1 to 5 years.
quotes.speaker: Andrew Yang

quotes.quote: Nobody is thinking this through for you. Do not rely on somebody else to give you the path forward.
quotes.speaker: Dave (DB2)

quotes.quote: Utopia is a deliberate choice.
quotes.speaker: Andrew Yang

quotes.quote: GDP per capita is $84,000 now, heading to $90,000 then $100,000 driven by AI productivity.
quotes.speaker: Andrew Yang
quotes.briu_use: Rising GDP chart with AI as inflection driver.

quotes.quote: Block shares surged 24% after laying off 4,000 employees.
quotes.speaker: Peter Diamandis
quotes.briu_use: Stock price vs headcount — the market incentive.

quotes.quote: It's either Star Trek or Mad Max. And you kind of veer towards one or the other.
quotes.speaker: Andrew Yang

quotes.quote: The success factors for our kids are the same as they ever were. It's grit, perseverance, coachability, sociability, caring about something. It's hustle.
quotes.speaker: Andrew Yang
quotes.briu_use: Human qualities still matter — AI amplifies them.

quotes.quote: Startups are pure utilization of the soul — energy, talent, optimism.
quotes.speaker: Andrew Yang

quotes.quote: The tailwind of abundance is overwhelmingly strong.
quotes.speaker: Peter Diamandis
